claim: Deep agents use four core components: a planning tool (to-do lists), sub-agents (specialized workers), file system access (context offloading), and detailed system prompts
actionable: Build agents with explicit planning tools, sub-agent orchestration, file-based memory, and comprehensive system prompts to handle long-horizon tasks

claim: Planning tools don't need to be functional (e.g., Claude's to-do-write is a no-op); they serve to generate messages in context that help the agent stay on track
actionable: Create minimal planning tools that output structured text to the model's context; doesn't require backend persistence

claim: Sub-agents preserve context by operating in isolated loops; their outputs don't pollute the main agent's context, allowing deep dives without losing focus
actionable: Delegate complex sub-tasks to isolated sub-agents with their own tool-calling loops; main agent orchestrates results without context bloat

claim: File systems offload context: instead of passing large observations directly, use short references (document X) and let agents read/write files deliberately
actionable: For long-running agents, write observations to files and reference them in context; prevents token bloat and preserves readability

claim: System prompts matter immensely: best deep agents have hundreds or thousands of lines of detailed prompts teaching the model how to use tools and plans
actionable: Don't skimp on system prompts for agents; invest in comprehensive docs on tool usage, task context, and behavioral guidelines
